subject: Advanced C++ Programming
description: Comprehensive C++ learning resource with key concepts, common errors, learning tips, and code examples for all advanced topics.

CPP-CORE-1.1.name: Compilation Pipeline
CPP-CORE-1.1.summary: C++ source code passes through four stages: preprocessing, compilation, assembly, and linking. Understanding this pipeline is essential for diagnosing build errors and structuring projects correctly.
keyConcepts: Preprocessor: Handles #include, #define, #ifdef — produces a single translation unit
Compiler: Converts each translation unit to an object file (.o or .obj)
Linker: Combines object files and resolves symbol references into an executable or library
One Definition Rule (ODR): Every symbol must have exactly one definition across all translation units
Declarations vs Definitions: A declaration says a symbol exists; a definition provides the implementation
Static vs Dynamic Linking: Static links at build time; dynamic links at runtime via .dll/.so
Link-Time Optimisation (LTO): Allows the linker to optimise across translation unit boundaries
commonErrors: Undefined reference errors — symbol declared but not defined or missing from linker command
Multiple definition errors — ODR violation when defining a function in a header included multiple times
Circular #include leading to incomplete types
Forgetting to link required libraries (e.g., -lm for math functions)
Mixing debug and release object files causing ABI mismatches
learningTips: Use `g++ -v` or `clang++ -v` to see the full compilation pipeline in action
Use `nm` or `objdump -t` to inspect symbols in object files
Use `ldd` on Linux or `dumpbin /dependents` on Windows to inspect shared library dependencies
Always put definitions in .cpp files and declarations in headers to avoid ODR violations
Use `#pragma once` or include guards in every header
socraticPrompts: Why does the linker complain about undefined references even when the function is declared in the header?
What does the ODR mean for template definitions — must they go in headers?
Why might two translation units define the same inline function without violating the ODR?
What is the difference between a static library and a shared library in terms of the pipeline?

CPP-CORE-1.2.name: Headers and Source Files
CPP-CORE-1.2.summary: Headers expose declarations that multiple translation units can share. Source files contain definitions. Include guards or #pragma once prevent duplicate inclusion within a single translation unit.
keyConcepts: #pragma once: Non-standard but universally supported single-include guard
Traditional include guards: #ifndef HEADER_H / #define HEADER_H / #endif
Forward declarations: Declare a class or function without defining it — avoids including heavy headers
Header-only libraries: All code in headers using inline or templates
Precompiled headers (PCH): Speed up builds by pre-compiling stable headers like <vector>
commonErrors: Missing include guards causing 'redefinition of class X' errors
Including a .cpp file instead of its header
Circular includes leading to incomplete type errors — use forward declarations instead
Defining non-inline functions in headers — violates ODR when included by multiple TUs
Forgetting to include the header that provides a type that the compiler inferred
learningTips: Prefer #pragma once for its simplicity and safety
Use forward declarations aggressively to keep headers minimal and compile times short
Organise headers as: system headers, then third-party, then project headers
IWYU (Include What You Use) is a tool that finds unnecessary or missing includes
socraticPrompts: When would you prefer a forward declaration over including the full header?
Why can inline functions be defined in headers without violating ODR?
What is the 'physical design' of a C++ project, and how do headers fit into it?

codeExample.description: Header with include guard and forward declaration

CPP-CORE-2.3.name: const, constexpr, and consteval
CPP-CORE-2.3.summary: const prevents runtime modification; constexpr allows or forces compile-time evaluation; consteval guarantees compile-time evaluation. Together they enable zero-overhead abstractions and compile-time computations.
keyConcepts: const: Object value cannot be changed after initialisation; applies to variables, member functions, and pointers
constexpr variable: Value computed at compile time; implicitly const
constexpr function: May be called at compile time (if inputs are constant) or at runtime
consteval (C++20): Function MUST be evaluated at compile time; called an 'immediate function'
constinit (C++20): Guarantees static initialisation at compile time; not const
mutable: Allows a member to be modified even in a const member function (e.g., caches)
commonErrors: Expecting constexpr to force compile-time evaluation — use consteval for that guarantee
Using constexpr with functions that call non-constexpr functions — compile error
Confusing const pointer vs pointer to const: int* const p vs const int* p
Forgetting to mark getter member functions as const — prevents calling on const objects
constinit does not mean const — the object can still be modified after initialisation
learningTips: Mark every getter and non-mutating function as const — it's part of a class's interface contract
Prefer constexpr to #define for constants — constexpr is type-safe and scoped
Use consteval for functions that must produce compile-time constants (e.g., string hashing)
Godbolt Compiler Explorer is excellent for verifying what gets computed at compile time
socraticPrompts: Why might you prefer constexpr over consteval for a math function?
What does it mean for a member function to be const, and what can it not do?
When would mutable be appropriate, and when would it be a design smell?

codeExample.description: const, constexpr, and consteval in action

CPP-CORE-3.4.name: Lambda Expressions
CPP-CORE-3.4.summary: Lambdas are anonymous function objects created inline. They can capture variables from the enclosing scope and are heavily used with STL algorithms, callbacks, and asynchronous code.
keyConcepts: [=] capture: Capture all local variables by value (copy)
[&] capture: Capture all local variables by reference
[x, &y] capture: Capture x by value, y by reference
[this] capture: Capture the enclosing object pointer
mutable lambda: Allows modification of captured-by-value variables within the lambda
Generic lambda (C++14): auto parameters create a templated call operator
std::function: Type-erased wrapper for any callable — has overhead; prefer auto or templates
commonErrors: Capturing a local variable by reference and using the lambda after the variable goes out of scope — dangling reference
Capturing 'this' in a lambda that outlives the object
Using std::function where auto would do — std::function has heap allocation overhead
Forgetting mutable when a by-value captured variable needs modification inside the lambda
learningTips: Default to capturing nothing [] and pass what you need as parameters
Use [&] only when the lambda definitely does not outlive the captured variables
Use auto for lambda variables — lambdas have unique unnamed types
Generic lambdas (auto params) are often preferable to std::function for performance
socraticPrompts: Why is capturing by reference dangerous for lambdas stored in a data structure?
What is the type of a lambda, and why can't you name it?
When would you prefer std::function over auto for a lambda variable?

codeExample.description: Lambda captures and use with STL algorithms

CPP-OOP-1.3.name: Rule of Zero, Three, and Five
CPP-OOP-1.3.summary: The Rule of Zero, Three, and Five govern when you must define special member functions. Violating these rules causes resource leaks, double frees, or dangling pointers.
keyConcepts: Rule of Zero: Prefer classes that manage no resources directly — let members (smart pointers, containers) handle it; define none of the five
Rule of Three (pre-C++11): If you define any of destructor, copy constructor, or copy assignment, define all three
Rule of Five (C++11+): If you define any of the three, also define move constructor and move assignment for efficiency
Copy constructor: T(const T& other) — deep copy of resources
Copy assignment: T& operator=(const T& other) — must handle self-assignment
Move constructor: T(T&& other) noexcept — steal resources from rvalue
Move assignment: T& operator=(T&& other) noexcept — steal resources, release old
= default and = delete: Explicitly request or forbid compiler-generated specials
commonErrors: Defining a destructor that frees a raw pointer but not defining a copy constructor — shallow copy leads to double free
Copy assignment that doesn't handle self-assignment (a = a) — undefined behaviour
Move constructor/assignment not marked noexcept — prevents use in STL containers during reallocation
Forgetting to null out the moved-from pointer — destructor runs on moved-from object and double-frees
learningTips: Aim for Rule of Zero by using RAII types (unique_ptr, vector, string) as members
When you must define one of the five, always define all five
Mark move constructor and move assignment noexcept — many STL optimisations depend on it
Use the copy-and-swap idiom for exception-safe copy assignment
socraticPrompts: Why does defining only a destructor lead to a shallow copy?
What is the copy-and-swap idiom, and how does it achieve exception safety?
Why must move constructors be noexcept for std::vector to use them during reallocation?

codeExample.description: Rule of Five for a resource-owning class

CPP-OOP-2.2.name: Virtual Functions and Dynamic Dispatch
CPP-OOP-2.2.summary: Virtual functions enable runtime polymorphism via a hidden vtable pointer. Every class with virtual functions has a vtable; calling a virtual function performs an indirect call through the vtable.
keyConcepts: virtual keyword: Marks a function for dynamic dispatch; derived classes can override it
override keyword: Explicitly states intent to override; compiler error if no matching virtual function found
final: Prevents further overriding (on a function) or inheritance (on a class)
Pure virtual (= 0): Makes the function abstract; the class cannot be instantiated
Virtual destructor: MUST be virtual if you delete a derived object via a base pointer — prevents resource leaks
vtable: Per-class table of function pointers built at compile time; each object with virtuals carries a vptr
Cost: One extra pointer per object (vptr); one level of indirection per virtual call
commonErrors: Non-virtual destructor in a base class — deleting through base pointer causes undefined behaviour
Forgetting override keyword — typos create a new function instead of overriding
Calling virtual functions in constructors or destructors — dynamic dispatch does not work there
Slicing: Assigning a derived object to a base object by value loses the derived part
learningTips: Always declare the base class destructor as virtual (or = default virtual ~Base())
Always use override on overriding functions — it catches interface mismatches at compile time
Prefer composition and non-virtual interfaces (NVI pattern) to limit the virtual surface
Use final to prevent accidental subclassing of classes not designed for it
socraticPrompts: Why is dynamic dispatch not possible in a constructor?
What would happen if you delete a derived object through a non-virtual base destructor?
What is the Non-Virtual Interface (NVI) pattern and what problem does it solve?

CPP-MEM-2.1.name: RAII Principle
CPP-MEM-2.1.summary: RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialisation) ties resource lifetimes to object lifetimes. Resources are acquired in constructors and released in destructors, guaranteeing cleanup even in the presence of exceptions.
keyConcepts: RAII: Resource lifetime == object lifetime; destructor always runs when object goes out of scope
Basic guarantee: No resource leaks after an exception, but object state may have changed
Strong guarantee: Either the operation succeeds completely or the state is unchanged (like a transaction)
Nothrow guarantee: Operation never throws — often required for move operations
Scope guard: A RAII wrapper that runs a lambda on scope exit regardless of exception
Typical RAII resources: memory, file handles, mutexes, network sockets, database connections
commonErrors: Acquiring resources with new/open without wrapping in RAII — resource leaks on exceptions
Using goto or longjmp — bypasses destructors, breaking RAII
Catching exceptions too broadly (catch ...) and swallowing without cleanup
Writing a destructor that can throw — undefined behaviour if destructor runs during stack unwinding
learningTips: Never use raw new/delete in application code — wrap in unique_ptr or a custom RAII class
Use std::lock_guard / std::scoped_lock instead of manually locking/unlocking mutexes
Destructors must be noexcept — an exception escaping a destructor during unwinding calls std::terminate
The standard library is built on RAII — study std::unique_ptr, std::fstream, std::lock_guard
socraticPrompts: What happens to destructors of already-constructed objects when an exception is thrown?
Why must destructors not throw, and what should you do if cleanup can fail?
How would you implement a scope guard using RAII and a lambda?

codeExample.description: RAII file handle and scope guard

CPP-MEM-2.2.name: Smart Pointers — unique_ptr
CPP-MEM-2.2.summary: std::unique_ptr provides exclusive ownership of a heap-allocated object. It has zero overhead compared to a raw pointer and automatically frees the object when it goes out of scope.
keyConcepts: Exclusive ownership: Only one unique_ptr owns a resource at a time
Non-copyable: unique_ptr cannot be copied; ownership is transferred via std::move
make_unique (C++14): Factory function — preferred over new; exception-safe
release(): Releases ownership without deleting; returns raw pointer (use carefully)
reset(p): Deletes current resource and takes ownership of p
get(): Returns raw pointer without releasing ownership
Custom deleter: unique_ptr<T, Deleter> for custom cleanup (e.g., fclose)
commonErrors: Initialising two unique_ptrs from the same raw pointer — double free when both destruct
Calling get() and using the raw pointer after the unique_ptr is destroyed — use-after-free
Using release() without immediately storing the returned pointer — memory leak
Passing unique_ptr by value without std::move — compile error (non-copyable)
learningTips: Always use make_unique — never write new in modern code
If a function takes unique_ptr by value, it takes ownership; by reference, it observes without taking ownership
unique_ptr<T[]> is the correct type for dynamic arrays — uses delete[] automatically
Functions should accept raw pointers or references, not smart pointers, unless ownership transfer is intended
socraticPrompts: Why is make_unique exception-safer than calling new directly?
What happens to the managed object when a unique_ptr is moved from?
When would you use a custom deleter with unique_ptr?

codeExample.output:
Widget 1 created
w owns Widget 1
Owns Widget 1
Widget 1 destroyed
w is empty

CPP-TPL-1.1.name: Function Templates
CPP-TPL-1.1.summary: Function templates generate a family of functions parameterised by type. The compiler deduces template arguments from the function call arguments and instantiates the appropriate version.
keyConcepts: Template parameter: Declared with template<typename T> or template<class T>
Template argument deduction: Compiler infers T from the call arguments automatically
Explicit instantiation: Specify T explicitly when deduction is ambiguous: max<double>(a, b)
Template specialisation: Provide a different implementation for a specific type
Non-type template parameters: template<int N> allows values as template arguments
Two-phase name lookup: Names in templates looked up at definition time and at instantiation time
commonErrors: Template definitions must be in headers — the compiler needs the full definition at every instantiation point
Ambiguous deduction when arguments of different types are passed: max(1, 2.0) — T can't be both int and double
Expecting operator+ to exist for all T — templates do not check until instantiated
Accidental shadowing: a local variable named the same as a template parameter
learningTips: Always put function template definitions in headers (or use explicit instantiation in a .cpp)
Use concepts (C++20) or static_assert to constrain template parameters and get clear error messages
Prefer templates over void* or virtual dispatch when types are known at compile time — zero overhead
Godbolt is invaluable for seeing what code templates generate
socraticPrompts: Why must template definitions reside in header files, unlike regular function definitions?
What is two-phase name lookup and why does it matter?
How do templates achieve zero-overhead abstraction compared to virtual dispatch?

codeExample.description: Function template with deduction and specialisation

CPP-MOV-1.2.name: Move Constructor and Move Assignment
CPP-MOV-1.2.summary: Move semantics allow resource transfer instead of copying. Move operations steal resources from rvalues (temporaries or objects cast with std::move), leaving the source in a valid but unspecified state.
keyConcepts: std::move: Casts an lvalue to an rvalue reference — does NOT actually move anything; it enables the move constructor/assignment to be called
Move constructor: T(T&& other) — takes ownership of other's resources; sets other's pointers to null
Move assignment: T& operator=(T&& other) — releases current resources, takes other's, nulls other
Moved-from state: A moved-from object must be in a valid (destructible) but unspecified state
noexcept move: Essential for STL containers to use move during reallocation instead of copy
commonErrors: Not marking move operations noexcept — std::vector uses copy instead of move on reallocation if move can throw
Accessing a moved-from object — valid to destroy or reassign, but not to read a meaningful value
Using std::move on a const object — calls copy instead of move silently
Returning a local variable with std::move — prevents NRVO and may be slower
learningTips: Mark all move constructors and move assignments noexcept — it's critical for performance with STL
Never return std::move(local_var) — the compiler applies NRVO automatically
std::move is just a cast; the actual resource transfer happens in the move constructor/assignment
Use std::exchange(ptr_, nullptr) in move constructors for clean pointer transfer
socraticPrompts: Why does std::move not actually move anything?
What guarantee must a moved-from object satisfy?
Why does std::vector use copy instead of move if the move constructor is not noexcept?

codeExample.description: Move semantics in a resource-owning class

CPP-CON-1.2.name: Mutual Exclusion
CPP-CON-1.2.summary: Mutexes prevent concurrent access to shared data. RAII wrappers (lock_guard, scoped_lock, unique_lock) ensure locks are always released, even when exceptions are thrown.
keyConcepts: std::mutex: Non-recursive mutual exclusion; lock() blocks until acquired
std::lock_guard: RAII mutex lock; locks on construction, unlocks on destruction — preferred for simple cases
std::unique_lock: More flexible than lock_guard; supports deferred locking, timed locking, and condition variables
std::scoped_lock (C++17): Locks multiple mutexes simultaneously using a deadlock-avoidance algorithm
std::recursive_mutex: Can be locked multiple times by the same thread; required for recursive algorithms
Deadlock: Thread A holds mutex 1 and waits for mutex 2; Thread B holds mutex 2 and waits for mutex 1
commonErrors: Locking mutex manually without RAII — lock is not released if exception is thrown
Locking mutexes in different orders in different threads — causes deadlock
Using std::mutex when you need std::shared_mutex for reader-writer locking
Locking the same non-recursive mutex twice from the same thread — deadlock
Accessing shared data after the lock_guard goes out of scope
learningTips: Always use RAII locks — never call mutex.lock() without a corresponding RAII wrapper
Use std::scoped_lock when acquiring multiple mutexes — it uses std::lock internally to avoid deadlock
Keep critical sections as short as possible — do not perform I/O or long operations while holding a lock
ThreadSanitizer (-fsanitize=thread) detects data races and lock order violations
socraticPrompts: Why is lock_guard preferred over calling mutex.lock() and mutex.unlock() manually?
How does std::scoped_lock avoid deadlock when locking multiple mutexes?
When would you choose unique_lock over lock_guard?

CPP-STL-3.4.name: Ranges and Views (C++20)
CPP-STL-3.4.summary: C++20 Ranges provide a composable, lazy pipeline for sequence processing. Views are range adaptors that transform or filter ranges without materialising intermediate results, enabling efficient and expressive data pipelines.
keyConcepts: std::ranges algorithms: Operate on range objects directly — no begin/end pair needed
std::views (range adaptors): filter, transform, take, drop, reverse, enumerate, zip
Pipe operator |: Composes views into a pipeline — evaluated lazily
Lazy evaluation: Elements are processed on demand — no intermediate containers allocated
std::ranges::to<> (C++23): Materialise a view into a container
commonErrors: Storing a view that outlives the underlying range — dangling view
Expecting views to be evaluated immediately — they are lazy; iteration triggers evaluation
Using views::filter with a stateful predicate that captures by reference after the range moves
Confusing std::views::transform with std::ranges::transform — former returns a lazy view, latter mutates in place
learningTips: Think of ranges as a functional pipeline: source | filter | transform | take
Views have zero allocation overhead — ideal for large datasets
std::ranges algorithms are safer — they accept ranges directly and cannot be called with mismatched begin/end
Use Compiler Explorer to verify that the ranges pipeline compiles to the same code as a hand-written loop
socraticPrompts: What is the difference between a range adaptor (view) and a range algorithm?
Why might a lazily-evaluated view be more memory-efficient than composing algorithms with intermediate vectors?
When would you avoid ranges in favour of classic iterator-pair algorithms?

codeExample.description: Composing views with the pipe operator

CPP-MOD20-2.2.name: Coroutines
CPP-MOD20-2.2.summary: C++20 coroutines are functions that can suspend and resume execution. They enable asynchronous programming and lazy generators without callback-based spaghetti code.
keyConcepts: co_await: Suspends the coroutine until an awaitable completes
co_yield: Suspends and produces a value (used in generators)
co_return: Finalises the coroutine and sets the return value
promise_type: The type that controls the coroutine's behaviour — produced by promise_type::get_return_object()
Awaitable: A type with operator co_await or the three await methods: await_ready, await_suspend, await_resume
Generator: A coroutine that yields a sequence of values lazily using co_yield
commonErrors: Forgetting that coroutines are stackless — cannot co_await inside a nested non-coroutine function
Lifetime issues: a coroutine's frame outlives local variables in the caller
Missing coroutine_handle::destroy() — coroutine frames are leaked like heap allocations
Trying to use C++20 coroutines without a proper executor or coroutine library — the low-level API is complex
learningTips: Start with a coroutine library (cppcoro, libcoro, or C++23 std::generator) before implementing promise_type from scratch
Think of co_await as 'pause here until this completes, then continue'
Coroutines are ideal for asynchronous I/O, lazy sequences, and state machines
std::generator (C++23) provides a ready-made generator type for co_yield-based iterators
socraticPrompts: What is the difference between a stackful coroutine (like a fiber) and a stackless C++20 coroutine?
How does co_yield differ from co_return?
Why must a coroutine's promise_type be customisable?
